# Binomial with a 3-level factor

What if the predictor is a category with **more than two** levels — say three
sites: **A**, **B**, **C**? You cannot code three groups with a single 0/1
column. You need a small **design matrix**.

## Reference (dummy) coding

Pick one level as the **reference** (say A). Create an indicator for each of the
others:

| Site | `is_B` | `is_C` |
|------|--------|--------|
| A    | 0 | 0 |
| B    | 1 | 0 |
| C    | 0 | 1 |

$$
\text{logit}(p_i) = \alpha + \beta_2\,\text{is\_B}_i + \beta_3\,\text{is\_C}_i
$$

- $\alpha$ — log-odds for the **reference** site A.
- $\beta_2$ — difference (B **vs** A).
- $\beta_3$ — difference (C **vs** A).

There is **no separate coefficient for A** — it is absorbed into the intercept.
That is why $k$ levels need $k-1$ indicator columns.

## In FANGS

If your CSV has a text `site` column with levels `A`, `B`, `C`, FANGS encodes it
to integers automatically, using the order of first appearance:

| Level | Code |
|-------|------|
| A | 1 |
| B | 2 |
| C | 3 |

You could build the `is_B`/`is_C` indicator columns yourself, but FANGS already
hands you the integer codes — so there is a neater way to write the model.

## Alternative parameterisation: index a coefficient vector

Instead of one intercept plus contrasts, give **each site its own coefficient**
and let FANGS's integer code pick the right one for each row. Because `site[i]`
is `1`, `2`, or `3`, the expression `gamma[site[i]]` selects `gamma[1]`,
`gamma[2]`, or `gamma[3]`:

$$
\text{logit}(p_i) = \gamma_{\,\text{site}_i}
$$

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    present[i] ~ dbern(p[i])
    logit(p[i]) <- gamma[site[i]]
  }
  for (k in 1:3) {
    gamma[k] ~ dnorm(0, 10)
  }
}
```

This is the **means (cell-means) parameterisation**: each $\gamma_k$ is the
log-odds of presence **at that site directly**, with no reference level. It maps
back to the dummy coding exactly:

- $\gamma_1 = \alpha$ — the reference site A.
- $\gamma_2 - \gamma_1 = \beta_2$ — the B-vs-A contrast.
- $\gamma_3 - \gamma_1 = \beta_3$ — the C-vs-A contrast.

The two models fit the same data and give the same predicted probabilities; they
just package the three numbers differently. Index coding is handy when you care
about **each level's value on its own** (and it scales to many levels without
writing an indicator column for each). Reference coding is handy when you care
about **differences from a baseline**, because the contrasts come straight out of
the summary table with their own credible intervals.

::: callout-tip
The `for (k in 1:3)` loop assigns the same prior to every site. Set the loop
bound (here `3`) to the number of levels in your factor — FANGS reports the
levels it found when you load the data.
:::

## Your task

Fit the 3-level model to `presence.csv`. Try it **both ways**:

1. **Reference coding** — read off the reference log-odds $\alpha$ and the contrasts $\beta_2$, $\beta_3$, and say which site each contrast compares against.
2. **Index coding** — fit the `gamma[site[i]]` model above and confirm that $\gamma_1 \approx \alpha$, $\gamma_2 - \gamma_1 \approx \beta_2$, and $\gamma_3 - \gamma_1 \approx \beta_3$.
